metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with a concrete oracle, e.g. a unit/integration test asserting the CLI output string for a grouped result contains the '(+N co-criteria: ...)' suffix) covering the CLI rendering change in src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py.
evidence: 'CLI renders the primary with a "(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)" suffix (src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py)'
ABSENCE: no AC or oracle command covers CLI output format
findings.finding: The CLI rendering change (the '(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix in src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py) is specified in 'What' but has no corresponding acceptance criterion or proving command, leaving it unverifiable.
findings.location: What section, bullet 4 (CLI renders the primary with a '(+N co-criteria: ...)' suffix); Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)
scenarios: An agent implements the grouping logic correctly but omits or mis-formats the CLI suffix; all existing ACs pass green while the user-facing output is wrong or absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'The CLI output for a grouped primary finding includes the suffix "(+N co-criteria: <union>)" where N equals the count of folded findings' criteria minus the primary's own. Oracle: unit test or `pytest tests/unit -k cli_group_suffix -x`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `norm_location` explicitly in the plan, specifying at minimum the normalization applied (e.g. `finding.get('location', '').strip().lower()` to match norm_id's case-insensitive approach), since no such function currently exists in the codebase.
evidence: search_files for 'norm_location' across all of src/rebar returns zero matches
sidecar.py's norm_id uses finding.get('location', '') directly without any normalization helper — there is no existing norm_location abstraction
Plan does not define what normalization norm_location applies, making the fix_unit_key's sensitivity to location format (trailing slashes, case, path separators) undefined
findings.finding: The plan references `norm_location(finding)` in the fix_unit_key formula as if it is an existing callable, but no `norm_location` function exists anywhere in the codebase. The plan does not specify what normalization `norm_location` applies (e.g. lowercasing, stripping whitespace, path canonicalization).
findings.location: What section: 'sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))'
scenarios: Two findings with the same conceptual location but trivially different string representations (e.g. 'What section' vs 'What Section' or 'sidecar.py' vs './sidecar.py') would hash to different fix_unit_keys, silently failing to group what should be grouped.
An implementer inventing norm_location independently may choose different normalization than what the plan intends, making the AC-2 unit-test assertions fragile.
findings.suggested_fix: In the fix_unit_key formula, replace `norm_location(finding)` with `finding.get('location', '').strip().lower()` (matching the case-insensitive token approach norm_id already uses for finding text) and document this inline rather than naming a nonexistent helper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly define what norm_location and norm_claim do (or confirm they are inline logic within fix_unit_key, not separate symbols), including their normalization contract — e.g. norm_location = lowercased, stripped location string; norm_claim = the same token-set normalization norm_id applies to the finding text.
evidence: Plan text: 'sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))' — uses norm_location and norm_claim as named callables
ABSENCE: grep across all of src/rebar returns zero hits for 'norm_location' or 'norm_claim'
The plan's Scope/IN section lists fix_unit_key as the new helper but does not list norm_location or norm_claim
findings.finding: The fix_unit_key formula references two sub-helpers, norm_location and norm_claim, as named function calls, but neither symbol exists anywhere in the codebase and neither is explicitly listed as a new artifact the plan creates. An implementer must infer they are to be invented inline or as unnamed sub-helpers, leaving the implementation boundary ambiguous.
findings.location: What section — fix_unit_key helper description: `sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))`
scenarios: An implementer treats norm_location/norm_claim as pre-existing and searches for them, finding nothing, then guesses at the normalization logic — potentially producing a different hash basis than intended.
Two implementers implement norm_location differently (e.g. lowercased vs. stripped vs. tokenized), breaking the grouping's determinism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'norm_location(finding) = finding.get("location", "").lower().strip(); norm_claim(finding) applies the same significant-token set normalization norm_id uses on finding text (reusing _NORM_STOP_TOKENS, re.findall, sorted set join) — these are inline expressions within fix_unit_key, not separately exported helpers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `norm_location` explicitly in the `fix_unit_key` spec — either apply the same token-set normalization as `norm_claim` (the simplest safe choice, since location strings are short and the same stop-token filter applies), or specify a concrete alternative (e.g. `strip().lower()`) and document the choice so the implementer doesn't have to guess.
evidence: Plan specifies `norm_claim applies the same token-set normalization norm_id uses on the finding text` — this is defined.
Plan specifies `norm_location(finding)` without any normalization description — this is absent.
norm_id in sidecar.py normalizes only `finding.get('finding', '')` (the claim text), not the location field; location is stored raw in `_slim()` as `f.get('location', '')`.
The motivating examples involve findings generated by independent LLM finder calls per criterion — location strings may differ in whitespace/punctuation across those calls.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `norm_location(finding)` as part of the `fix_unit_key` hash basis but never defines its normalization behavior — whether it applies the same token-set lowercasing as `norm_claim`, a simple `.strip().lower()`, or an exact string match. The motivating use case (same defect surfaced by multiple criteria) requires that location strings produced by independent finder calls for the same underlying issue hash identically. If two findings cite the same location with minor formatting differences (trailing whitespace, mixed case, punctuation), `fix_unit_key` will differ and the grouping silently fails to fire.
findings.location: What section: `fix_unit_key(finding)` helper definition — `sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))`
scenarios: Two criteria fire on the same missing symbol; criterion A's finder emits location='## What' and criterion B's finder emits location='## What ' (trailing space) — fix_unit_key differs, grouping silently misses, block count stays at 2.
A finder emits 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py' and another emits 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:42' — different keys, no grouping.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same token-set normalization to the location string as `norm_claim` uses on the finding text: `sorted({t for t in re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', location.lower()) if len(t) > _NORM_STOP_TOKENS})`, joined and hashed. This reuses existing machinery and tolerates minor formatting variation in LLM-generated location strings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `norm_location(finding)` explicitly in the plan — specify whether it lowercases, strips line/column suffixes (e.g. `:L10`, `:10`), strips trailing punctuation, and handles `None`/empty — so the fix_unit_key formula is fully specified and the grouping boundary is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan states the key is `sha256(norm_location(finding) + '|' + norm_claim(finding))` but provides no definition of `norm_location`.
Search of `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` confirms `norm_location` does not exist — only `norm_id` is defined (sidecar.py:274).
The existing `norm_id` does not normalize the `location` field at all; `norm_location` would require new design decisions (strip line numbers? lowercase? path separators? handle None?).
Without a `norm_location` spec, two findings citing `src/foo.py:L10` vs `src/foo.py` vs `src/foo.py:10` could group or not depending on implementation — a silent correctness risk in the grouping key.
findings.finding: `norm_location(finding)` appears in the `fix_unit_key` formula but does not exist in the codebase and is not specified in the plan — it is an unverified capability assertion masking a design gap.
findings.location: ## What — fix_unit_key formula: `sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))`
scenarios: A finding with location `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:L274` and another with `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` — same file, different specificity — may or may not group depending on how `norm_location` is implemented, yielding inconsistent deduplication.
A location of `None` or `''` for a finding with no location anchor — the formula concatenates `norm_location(finding)` directly, so a missing/None location must be handled or it crashes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph spec alongside the `fix_unit_key` bullet: `norm_location(f)` lowercases `f.get('location', '') or ''`, strips any trailing `:(L?\d+)` line-number suffix, and strips surrounding whitespace — matching the same normalization `norm_id` implicitly applies to `finding` text. This should be captured in a docstring alongside the implementation and tested explicitly in the AC-2 unit tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `norm_location`'s normalization contract explicitly in the plan (e.g. 'lowercase the raw location string, strip leading/trailing whitespace; do NOT apply token-set normalization') so implementors have an unambiguous specification.
evidence: Plan text: 'sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))'
grep for 'norm_location' across all of src/rebar returns zero matches — the function does not exist

norm_id (the existing helper) applies lowercased alphanumeric token-set normalization; norm_location's contract is unspecified — is it a raw lowercased string, a token-set like norm_claim, or something else?
findings.finding: `norm_location` is named in the fix_unit_key formula but never defined — its normalization contract (casing, tokenization, whitespace handling) is completely absent from the plan.
findings.location: What section: 'Add a `fix_unit_key(finding)` helper in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py'
scenarios: Two findings with location 'src/foo.py' vs 'src/foo.py ' (trailing space) produce different keys under a raw-string norm_location, grouping fails silently.
An implementor writing norm_location must guess whether to apply token-set normalization (like norm_claim) or simple lowercasing — different choices produce incompatible keys across implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'norm_location(finding): lowercase(finding.get("location", "").strip()) — a simple whitespace-normalized lowercase string (not token-set); two findings at the same path/line string will share a key.'

ACs 1–3 all target unit tests over build_payload output and fix_unit_key; none assert CLI output format or content

findings.finding: The CLI rendering behavior — the '(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix added in src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py — has no acceptance criterion or test covering its output. This is a new user-facing formatted output with zero specified test coverage.

findings.finding: The CLI rendering deliverable — 'CLI renders the primary with a (+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...) suffix' — is described in the What section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it. No test, oracle, or pass condition is defined for the CLI output change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan whether `coverage.counts.blocking` reflects the pre-grouping count (all 5 findings) or the post-grouping primary count (1), and whether the CLI `blocking=N` display reflects primaries or the full count.
evidence: orchestrator.py finalize_verdict line 507: 'coverage["counts"] = {"blocking": len(blocking), ...}' — counts are set from the full blocking list length
AC1: 'a synthetic verdict with 5 blocking findings... surfaces exactly 1 primary' — but the plan is silent on whether counts.blocking becomes 1 or stays 5
The CLI renderer (_render_plan_review_text) prints counts.get('blocking', 0) — if it stays 5 but only 1 primary is shown, the count/display is inconsistent
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `coverage.counts.blocking` should be updated (or whether it should remain unchanged) after grouping reduces the surfaced blocking list from N primaries to fewer — leaving an ambiguous counter placement.
findings.location: What section (grouping description) and Scope section: 'IN: fix_unit_key helper, grouping of BLOCKING findings only'
scenarios: counts.blocking=5 but only 1 primary rendered → confusing agent-facing output

counts.blocking=1 → downstream calibration consumers using counts for FP analysis see different blocking tallies than the actual sidecar findings count, breaking the calibration purpose the plan cites

findings.finding: The `members[]` field placed on the primary finding is never defined structurally: no field list, no type (list of finding ids? list of full finding dicts? list of {id, criteria} pairs?). The plan says 'folded findings listed on the primary as members[]' but provides no schema. The sidecar `_slim()` function in `sidecar.py` has an explicit field-selection list — adding a nested `members[]` with undefined structure leaves a structural gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group … with its co-criteria and the folded findings listed on the primary as members[]'

evidence: Plan text: 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority), carrying members[] (the folded findings)'
orchestrator.py line 406: DET blocking findings are all assigned priority=1.0
LLM blocking findings can also share the same computed priority score
No stable secondary sort key (e.g. insertion order, id, criteria count) is named
findings.finding: The tiebreak rule for selecting the 'highest priority' primary within a fix-unit group is unspecified when multiple blocking findings in the group share identical priority values (e.g. all DET-tier blocking findings are hardcoded to priority=1.0).
findings.location: What section: 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority)'
scenarios: A group of 5 findings all with priority=1.0 — which becomes primary? Unstable sort order produces non-deterministic output across Python versions or runs, making the AC unit test flaky.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether coverage.counts['blocking'] should be updated to reflect the post-grouping primary count (e.g. 1 primary with 4 members) or left as the raw pre-group total, and add a coverage field (e.g. 'blocking_grouped': true, 'blocking_raw_count': 5) so consumers can distinguish.
evidence: coverage.counts['blocking'] is written in orchestrator.finalize_verdict before _run_plan_review applies grouping

_render_plan_review_text line 530-534 prints 'blocking={counts.get("blocking", 0)}' and then iterates verdict['blocking'] — if grouping reduces blocking[] to 1 primary but counts['blocking'] still says 5, the summary is contradictory
Plan does not mention updating counts after grouping
findings.finding: The plan groups blocking findings and surfaces ONE primary per group in verdict['blocking'], but does not mention updating coverage.counts['blocking'] (set during partition_findings/finalize_verdict before the grouping step). After grouping, the count field still reflects the pre-group total (e.g. 5), while only 1 primary is rendered — the CLI's 'blocking=5' summary line will contradict the single '[BLOCK ...]' line shown.

Downstream consumers that read coverage.counts['blocking'] for numeric comparisons (e.g. calibration, audit UIs) see the pre-group count and cannot reconcile it with the grouped blocking[] list length.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `members[]` on the primary finding is (a) persisted via `_slim()` (in which case add it explicitly alongside `group_id`) or (b) runtime-only/CLI-only (in which case state that explicitly so the executor doesn't add it to _slim()).

'explicitly add group_id to the _slim() dict in sidecar.py' — members[] is not mentioned alongside group_id in _slim()'s update

findings.finding: The plan states the primary finding carries a `members[]` field but never specifies whether `members[]` is added to `_slim()` / persisted in `build_payload`, leaving an ambiguity about whether downstream replay consumers can access member finding details from the sidecar artifact.
findings.location: What section, bullet 2 ('Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group … carrying members[]'); What section, bullet 3 ('explicitly add group_id to the _slim() dict')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the behavioral change to primary-findings surfacing (N→1 per group), enumerate known pipeline consumers, and add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing.
evidence: What says: 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority), carrying members[] (the folded findings)'

The plan is silent on whether any caller of the plan-review pipeline (other than the CLI and sidecar) iterates blocking findings by count or by list, and provides no rollback/back-out plan for this behavioral change.
Scope section lists no consumer-acknowledgement or migration step for pipeline callers.
findings.finding: The plan changes the effective pipeline output shape — N blocking findings collapse to 1 primary (with members[]) visible to upstream consumers — but provides no rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or acknowledgement of callers that iterate the primary findings list expecting all N entries.
findings.location: What (grouping seam in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py) / Scope
scenarios: A caller in the rebar pipeline counts len(blocking_findings) to gate an action — post-change it sees 1 instead of 5, silently changing gate behavior.
A replay/audit tool joins on the findings list and misses grouped findings because it doesn't know about members[]; no expand-contract or version bump signals the schema change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Compat & Rollback' subsection in What: list known callers of the plan-review pipeline that consume blocking findings; confirm they handle members[] or are updated in-scope; specify that the change is revertable by removing the grouping step (no data migration needed since sidecar retains all findings).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: A bug in the grouping loop (e.g., it only processes the first group and drops the second) passes AC1 but fails in production when a verdict has two co-cited defects

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section (or inline schema changelog) documenting the `group_id` field contract, primary/member semantics, and the fix_unit_key construction so replay and calibration consumers have a durable specification.
evidence: 'folded findings keep decision=block in the sidecar (calibration data lossless) and gain a `group_id` field (= the fix_unit_key) so replay can join groups'
'explicitly add `group_id` to the `_slim()` dict' — schema change with no schema changelog or design doc
ABSENCE: no mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The primary/member semantics and the rule 'highest priority' for primary selection are novel architectural decisions not captured in any durable artifact named in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline-layer contract (group_id field on sidecar schema, primary/member semantics, fix_unit_key as a cross-criterion join key) with no corresponding ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry, leaving downstream consumers (replay, calibration) without a durable specification of the grouping semantics.
findings.location: ## What (all sub-bullets) and ## Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: A future engineer modifies the sidecar schema without knowing group_id has join semantics for replay
A calibration consumer reads block findings and misinterprets folded members as independent blocking findings because the grouping contract is undocumented
The 'highest priority' primary-selection rule is silently changed, breaking group semantics for downstream consumers
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Design note` subsection to the plan (or a short entry in CLAUDE.md / docs/adr/) covering: (1) the fix_unit_key schema (sha256 of norm_location + '|' + norm_claim, criteria-free), (2) the primary-selection rule ('highest priority' — define what field), (3) the group_id sidecar field contract (all members in a group share the same group_id value), and (4) that folded members retain decision=block for calibration fidelity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the CLI rendering bullet whether folded/member findings are suppressed from CLI display (shown only via the '+N co-criteria' suffix on the primary) or rendered separately, and add an AC asserting the CLI output count for the 5-finding synthetic case.

CLI renders the primary with a '(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix — no mention of suppressing or hiding folded findings from display
Title: 'one defect, one block group' implies the agent sees one entry, not five with one decorated
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether folded (member) blocking findings are suppressed from CLI output; if they are rendered alongside the primary, the stated goal of 'one defect, one block group' (the reduced block wall) is not achieved at the user-visible layer.
findings.location: What section — 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group'; CLI rendering bullet (src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py)
scenarios: Agent is shown all 5 blocking findings in CLI output; the inflated block wall problem (the stated motivation) is not fixed despite the grouping logic being correct internally.
Sidecar shows 5 block decisions correctly, but CLI still presents 5 lines, defeating the UX purpose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CLI bullet: 'folded findings are omitted from CLI output; only the primary is rendered with the (+N co-criteria: ...) suffix.' Add AC: 'CLI output for the 5-finding synthetic case contains exactly 1 blocking line.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CLI rendering spec (or an AC) for the no-members case (suppress suffix), the non-blocking/no-group_id case, and legacy findings missing group_id.
evidence: CLI bullet only describes the happy-path case: '(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix

Grouping is BLOCKING-only, so non-blocking findings in the same output will lack group_id
findings.finding: The CLI rendering spec does not address edge cases: (a) a group with exactly 1 finding (no members, suffix should be absent), (b) non-blocking findings that never receive a group_id (suffix logic must not apply), and (c) the case where group_id is absent on legacy/replayed findings.
findings.location: CLI rendering bullet (src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py); Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: CLI crashes or shows '(+0 co-criteria:)' for a solo blocking finding with no peers.
Non-blocking findings accidentally receive suffix rendering if the condition checks group_id presence rather than members length.
Replayed old sidecar findings (no group_id field) cause KeyError or display corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the CLI bullet: 'Suffix is omitted when members[] is empty or absent. Non-blocking findings and findings without group_id are rendered without a suffix. Missing group_id is treated as ungrouped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which consumers read group_id (replay, CLI, any analytics) and specify that each treats absence of group_id as 'ungrouped' (None/missing is valid), or add an AC asserting a mixed blocking+advisory payload round-trips without error.
evidence: 'additive optional field on the current plan-review sidecar schema' — stated for the schema, but consumers are not listed as tolerant of absence
Only blocking findings receive group_id; non-blocking findings in the same payload will not have it
AC3 only tests that 5 block findings share one group_id; it does not assert that advisory/non-blocking findings in the same payload are handled without error
findings.finding: The plan does not specify consumer tolerance for absence of group_id on non-blocking findings or on findings from sidecars written before this change; replay and any downstream consumers of build_payload output may not handle the field's absence gracefully.
findings.location: Sidecar persistence bullet; '_slim()' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py; AC3
scenarios: Replay logic that joins on group_id raises AttributeError/KeyError for advisory findings in the same sidecar.
A future consumer iterates all findings and unconditionally reads group_id, breaking on pre-existing sidecars.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sidecar persistence bullet: 'group_id is None/absent for non-blocking findings; all consumers must treat absence as ungrouped.' Extend AC3 to include advisory findings in the synthetic payload and assert they have no group_id and cause no error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `norm_claim` explicitly in the plan (or rename it to a concrete inline description), specifying that it IS the same `re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', text.lower())` + `len(t) > _NORM_STOP_TOKENS` filter already in `norm_id`, and clarify that the implementation should factor this shared extraction into a private helper (e.g. `_sig_tokens(text)`) called by both `norm_id` and `fix_unit_key` — since `norm_claim` does not currently exist in the codebase.
evidence: search_files for 'norm_claim' across all of src/rebar returns zero matches

norm_id in sidecar.py lines 279-280 has inline logic: tokens = sorted({t for t in re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', text) if len(t) > _NORM_STOP_TOKENS}) — there is no separately extractable norm_claim function

Plan says 'norm_claim applies the same token-set normalization norm_id uses on the finding text (reusing its stop-token filtering) but EXCLUDES the criteria list' — implying reuse of an existing abstraction that does not exist
findings.finding: The plan references `norm_claim` (in the fix_unit_key formula: `norm_claim applies the same token-set normalization norm_id uses on the finding text (reusing its stop-token filtering)`) as if it is an existing, reusable helper, but no such function exists anywhere in the codebase. The plan's phrasing 'reusing its stop-token filtering' implies the normalization logic is already factored out and callable by name, but `norm_id`'s token-set logic is entirely inline (a single expression on lines 279-280 of sidecar.py) with no separately named `norm_claim` extraction.
findings.location: What section: 'Add a fix_unit_key(finding) helper… norm_claim applies the same token-set normalization norm_id uses'
scenarios: An implementer reading 'reusing its stop-token filtering' will look for norm_claim or a factored helper and find nothing, leading to either duplicating the logic (drift risk) or silently re-implementing it differently (semantic gap in the criteria-free key).
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace the reference to `norm_claim` with: 'extract a private `_sig_tokens(text: str) -> list[str]` helper from the inline logic in `norm_id` (the `re.findall` + stop-token filter on lines 279-280 of sidecar.py), then define `fix_unit_key` as `sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + " ".join(_sig_tokens(finding.get("finding", ""))))`, reusing `_sig_tokens` in `norm_id` as well.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py as a consumer of verdict['blocking'] and either confirm the members[] shape change is acceptable on the MCP surface or scope it to CLI/sidecar only (e.g. only add members[] in the CLI renderer, not in the verdict dict itself).
evidence: src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:187 — 'Run the plan-review gate on a ticket -> a plan_review_verdict dict' returned to MCP callers
The plan's Scope section names only CLI, sidecar, and tests — no mention of MCP consumers
members[] on a blocking finding is a new field not previously present in the MCP-surfaced verdict
findings.finding: src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py is an unenumerated consumer of review_plan's verdict dict: it returns the verdict directly to MCP callers. If verdict['blocking'] items gain members[] (folded findings), the MCP output shape changes for grouped findings — this is an out-of-plan consumer that may surprise MCP clients.
findings.location: Scope/IN section; src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py (review_plan MCP tool)
scenarios: An MCP client inspects blocking[0] and encounters an unexpected members[] array — may cause a schema mismatch or silent data confusion in client-side parsers.
An agent tool that iterates blocking[] and counts findings sees only 1 item where 5 were expected, and misinterprets the dedup as the review having fewer problems.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the leaf's parent ticket explicitly scopes fix_unit_key grouping, members[], group_id persistence, and the CLI suffix — if the parent is 5fd2 (which specifies none of these), update the parent's plan first to authorize this capability before re-reviewing the leaf under G7.
evidence: Parent 5fd2 plan has no mention of 'fix_unit_key', 'cross-criterion grouping', 'members[]', 'group_id', or a CLI suffix for grouped findings
Leaf What: 'Add a fix_unit_key(finding) helper ... sha256(norm_location(finding) + "|" + norm_claim(finding))' — absent from parent

Leaf What: 'group blocking findings sharing a fix_unit_key. Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority), carrying members[] ... and the union of co-criteria' — absent from parent
Leaf What: 'explicitly add group_id to the _slim() dict in sidecar.py' — absent from parent
Leaf What: 'CLI renders the primary with a "(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)" suffix' — absent from parent
Parent 5fd2 iteration-fatigue section discusses advisory cap and never-drop-blocking as mitigations but specifies no blocking-finding grouping
Parent 5fd2 observability section covers the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar schema but specifies no group_id field
findings.finding: The leaf introduces fix_unit_key grouping of blocking findings (fix_unit_key helper, members[] field, group_id in the sidecar, CLI suffix '(+N co-criteria:...)') — deliverables that do not appear anywhere in the parent epic 5fd2's plan. The parent's design covers the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion calibration, the advisory cap, and the iteration-fatigue failure mode, but specifies no cross-criterion dedup key, no blocking-finding grouping mechanism, no group_id sidecar field, and no grouped-finding CLI rendering. The leaf is adding net-new capabilities to a closed parent, not narrowing any parent-specified deliverable.
findings.location: Whole plan (What, Scope, Acceptance Criteria); parent epic 5fd2 plan (Design summary, Observability/FP substrate, Iteration-fatigue failure mode section)
scenarios: If the leaf's parent is 5fd2 (closed), the leaf is delivering out-of-scope work relative to the parent's plan — the parent must be updated first to authorize the grouping capability, or a different authorized parent must be cited.
If there is a different parent ticket that explicitly scopes this fix-unit grouping work, the G7 check should be re-run against that parent.
findings.suggested_fix: If 5fd2 is the parent: add a brief 'calibration-dedup follow-on' section to 5fd2's plan (or a new authorized child-of-5fd2 epic entry) that explicitly scopes fix_unit_key grouping of blocking findings, the group_id sidecar field, and the CLI rendering change, then re-review this leaf as a consistent narrowing of that scoped work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `_cluster_findings` in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py` in the plan and explicitly document how the plan-review grouping differs (hash-based vs equality-based key, post-Pass-3 vs pre-Pass-2 placement, blocking-only scope) and why the existing implementation is not reused — confirming the design is a justified specialization, not an oversight.
evidence: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:145` defines `_cluster_findings` — a location+criterion-keyed grouping that produces `members[]`, a representative finding, `agreement` count, and `merged_from` for non-lossy text preservation.
The plan's grouping concept (one primary per group, carrying `members[]`, union of co-criteria) is structurally identical to `_cluster_findings`'s output shape.
Plan section '## What' introduces `members[]` and 'highest priority' representative selection without referencing this existing pattern.

The code-review implementation already resolved subtle edge cases: what to do when a finding has no location or no criterion (fallback to text-based key); how to preserve non-representative finding text non-lossily.
findings.finding: The plan does not reference the directly analogous `_cluster_findings` prior art in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py`, which already implements post-pass finding grouping with `members[]`, representative selection, non-lossy text preservation (`merged_from`), and cluster-key fallback — design decisions the plan will need to independently re-solve.
findings.location: ## What — grouping of blocking findings / members[] / primary selection
scenarios: The plan's `members[]` field will need the same non-lossy text preservation decision the code-review path solved with `merged_from` — if the plan omits this, folded findings' `finding` text is unrecoverable from the primary alone.

If a blocking finding has an empty location, `norm_location('')` as a group key may accidentally cluster unrelated findings that also lack a location — the code-review implementation has an explicit fallback for this case that the plan does not mention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all `group_id` consumers/readers must treat the field as optional (e.g., `.get('group_id')` / `Optional[str]`) and document the expected schema state (field absent vs. null) for pre-migration sidecar records so replay is safe against historical data.
evidence: 'replay can join groups' — plan implicitly assumes group_id is always present in sidecar records
'additive optional field on the current plan-review sidecar schema' — plan acknowledges optionality at write time but does not prescribe read-time handling
ABSENCE: no fallback/default for group_id=None on old records is specified anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan states 'replay can join groups' via `group_id` but does not specify how replay/consumer logic handles pre-existing sidecar records that lack the `group_id` field entirely — no default value, no `None`-guard, and no migration of historical records is described. If the consumer unconditionally accesses `group_id`, it will raise a KeyError/AttributeError on any sidecar written before this change is deployed.
findings.location: What section — `_slim()`/`build_payload` description; 'replay can join groups' phrase
scenarios: A replay job runs after partial deployment, reading a mix of old (no group_id) and new (has group_id) sidecar records — old records cause an unhandled missing-key error.
A rollback of the code change leaves previously written group_id values in sidecar records; re-deployment reads them fine, but an intermediate reader written before the guard is added may crash.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's `_slim()` / `build_payload` section, add: 'All readers of `group_id` MUST use a safe-access pattern (e.g., `record.get("group_id")`) and treat absence as `None`; no migration of historical sidecar records is performed, so `group_id` will be absent on pre-existing entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan whether the grouping step stamps `group_id` by mutating finding dicts in-place on `verdict['blocking']` before `sidecar.emit()`, and whether the primary replaces the full blocking list or the full list is preserved with group_id annotations.
evidence: sidecar.py build_payload line 421: 'all_findings = verdict.get("blocking", []) + verdict.get("advisory", []) + ...' — _slim(f) is called on each element of these lists
For group_id to survive into _slim(), it must exist as a key on each `f` dict at the time build_payload is called
The plan says grouping runs 'before the sidecar emit' but does not say whether the grouping step mutates the finding dicts in verdict['blocking'] in-place, or builds a parallel structure
findings.finding: The plan does not specify WHERE `group_id` is stamped on individual finding objects so that `_slim()` / `build_payload` can read it — the grouping logic mutates (or wraps) the `verdict['blocking']` list, but the plan does not say whether findings are mutated in-place on the verdict dict or whether `build_payload` receives a separate annotated structure.
findings.location: What section: 'Sidecar persistence: build_payload's _slim() field-selection currently omits unknown fields — explicitly add group_id to the _slim() dict'
scenarios: If grouping builds a new primary-findings list separate from verdict['blocking'] without stamping group_id on the original findings, folded members in verdict['blocking'] never get group_id and AC3 fails.

If grouping mutates verdict['blocking'] in-place, the surfaced verdict shape changes (primary replaces all 5 entries), which may break the plan_review_verdict schema validation at the end of _run_plan_review.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria', but reading the implementation shows norm_id is UNCONDITIONALLY criterion-scoped — criteria are always baked into the hash (`basis = tokens + "|" + sorted_criteria`). Two findings with identical location and claim text but different criteria will always produce different norm_id values. The function can never 'match across criteria'. The plan hedges with 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)' — but since norm_id is ALWAYS criterion-scoped, the reuse path described first is unreachable and misleading. An implementer reading the plan could attempt to call norm_id as the grouping key and find it never groups multi-criterion findings.
findings.location: What section / 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grouping-key description with: 'Derive the fix-unit key as `(normalized_location, claim_token_set)` where claim_token_set reuses sidecar._NORM_STOP_TOKENS filtering on the finding text but EXCLUDES criteria from the hash — this is a new helper alongside norm_id, not norm_id itself (norm_id is unconditionally criterion-scoped and cannot serve as a cross-criterion grouping key).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts it will 'reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in sidecar.py' for cross-criterion fix-unit grouping, but immediately hedges with 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)' — deferring resolution of which path actually applies to an inline implementation note. This is an unverified internal capability assertion: the plan never confirms whether norm_id in sidecar.py is or is not criterion-scoped, leaving the core algorithmic path unresolved at plan time.
findings.location: What section — 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery … where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note.'
findings.suggested_fix: Open sidecar.py, check norm_id's signature and call sites. If criterion-scoped: specify the fix-unit key as `hash(normalize(location) + normalize(core_claim))` (independent of criterion) and remove the reuse option. If cross-criterion: confirm reuse and delete the alternative branch. Either way, the plan should contain one authoritative sentence, not a fork deferred to an inline note.

findings.finding: `norm_id` in `sidecar.py` is criterion-SCOPED (its hash includes the sorted criteria list), so two findings for the same defect at the same location but citing different criteria will produce different `norm_id` values and cannot be grouped by `norm_id` equality. The plan proposes 'reuse the existing ... norm_id machinery' for the fix-unit key, then acknowledges the fallback to '(location, claim-normalization)', but does not commit to which path is actually taken. A new cross-criteria key (stripped of criteria from the hash basis) is needed, and that is a new symbol, not a reuse.
findings.location: What section: 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py ... where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `fix_unit_key(finding)` helper in `sidecar.py` that computes the same token-set hash as `norm_id` but WITHOUT appending the criteria, and call that the grouping key. Name it in the plan as a new edit target in `sidecar.py`.

findings.finding: `sidecar.build_payload` / its inner `_slim()` function in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` must be modified to persist `group_id` (and optionally `members[]`) on findings for AC3 ('unit test reads the emitted sidecar dict and asserts 5 block-decision findings and a shared group id'), but `build_payload`/`sidecar.py` is not listed as an edit target in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section (IN: 'sidecar-lossless') and AC3

findings.finding: The grouping key decision is explicitly deferred ('the implementation must state which in an inline design note') but the plan itself never resolves it. Two incompatible candidates are named — reuse criterion-scoped `norm_id` (which folds criteria into the hash, so two findings with different criteria but the same claim get different keys) vs derive a location+claim-only key (which ignores criteria, grouping across all criteria as intended) — and the plan picks neither. An implementer cannot produce a deterministic grouping without this choice.
findings.location: What section — 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria; where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous 'reuse norm_id … OR derive …' clause with: 'Derive the fix-unit key as `fix_unit_key(f) = sha256(norm_location(f.location) + "|") + norm_claim(f.finding))` where `norm_claim` applies the same token-set approach as `norm_id` but WITHOUT the criteria suffix — so findings sharing the same defect at the same location group regardless of which criterion surfaces them.'

findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'a shared group id' in the sidecar, but no `group_id` (or equivalent) field is defined for the sidecar payload. The existing `_slim()` function in `sidecar.py` has a fixed field-selection list; `group_id` is absent from it. Without explicitly adding `group_id` to `_slim()` and `build_payload`, the AC3 oracle test would read `None` for every finding.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 — 'unit test reads the emitted sidecar dict and asserts 5 block-decision findings and a shared group id'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"group_id": f.get("group_id")` to the `_slim()` dict in `sidecar.py:375-419` and name this field explicitly in the plan's What section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
